title: Sparse distribution problems for some dynamical systems
speaker: Adam Kanigowski
abstract: Given a topological dynamical system (X,T) a bounded sequence (an) and f∈C(X) we are interested in the asymptotic behavior of 1∑n≤N|an|∑n≤Nanf(Tnx)

proof_stages.label: Unique ergodicity as the ordinary-average baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from the von Neumann and Birkhoff ergodic theorems and passes to topological systems, where unique ergodicity is the condition giving uniform convergence of all continuous ordinary orbit averages.

proof_stages.label: Sparse weighted averages and everywhere questions
proof_stages.summary: The central object is a normalized weighted average of f(T^n x), with weights or sparse supports coming from arithmetic sequences, and the main topological question is whether convergence or zero limiting average holds for every initial point in a uniquely ergodic system.

proof_stages.label: Möbius case via zero entropy and fixed-scale joinings
proof_stages.summary: For multiplicative weights, especially the Möbius function, Sarnak's zero-entropy conjecture gives the desired dynamical condition, while the Kátai--Bourgain--Sarnak--Ziegler criterion reduces many proofs to fixed-prime type-II correlations interpretable as joinings of T^p and T^q.

proof_stages.label: Prime averages and the large-scale type-II obstruction
proof_stages.summary: For averages along primes, the dynamical prime number theorem is formulated and known examples are surveyed, but unlike the Möbius case the required type-II estimates involve p and q growing with N, so ordinary joining methods no longer directly apply.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity and polynomial approximation for prime averages
proof_stages.summary: A strong prime-time rigidity hypothesis T^{q_n} close to the identity yields prime distribution along a subsequence by splitting primes into residue classes and invoking unique ergodicity, and smoother skew products are treated by replacing exact rigidity with polynomial-phase approximation and short-interval prime estimates.

proof_stages.label: Semiprime averages and small-scale type-II criterion
proof_stages.summary: For products of two primes, a recent criterion reduces semiprime orbit distribution to quantitative type-II correlations in which p and q are only very small powers or even logarithmic in N, making dynamical estimates much more accessible than for primes.

proof_stages.label: Effective Ratner input for horocycle semiprime distribution
proof_stages.summary: Recent effective Ratner bounds of Lindenstrauss--Mohammadi--Wang for product horocycle correlations, away from explicit exceptional cases, are proposed as the dynamical input needed to prove semiprime distribution results for horocycle-type systems.

examples.name: Bernoulli shift as non-unique-ergodic contrast
examples.note: The Bernoulli shift is mentioned only to illustrate how many invariant measures can lead to different orbit statistics when unique ergodicity fails.
examples.engagement_reason: The Bernoulli shift is only cited as a contrast to unique ergodicity, with no analysis carried out on it.

examples.name: Irrational rotations as tractable test systems
examples.note: Rotations illustrate the spectral-theoretic reduction of L2 questions and later serve as a familiar class where prime averages are understood, with characters simplifying type-II expressions.
examples.engagement_reason: For rotations, the speaker explains how characters make the type-two sums collapse into a type-one sum involving a p−q frequency, so the criterion is concretely traced in this case.

passes.reason: Irrational rotations are homogeneous isometric systems with high regularity, used as tractable positive test cases.

examples.name: Diagonal obstruction x=y in product horocycle correlations
examples.note: Taking the two product-flow points equal shows why effective Ratner-type correlation bounds must exclude explicit exceptional configurations rather than hold uniformly for all pairs.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the case x=y in product horocycle correlations to explain why uniform bounds cannot hold, explicitly reasoning through this obstruction.

passes.reason: The diagonal case x=y collapses the product correlation to a self-correlation and obstructs uniform decay bounds.
The diagonal pair x=y collapses the two product coordinates and obstructs uniform horocycle correlation decay.

the diagonal case x=y is the perfectly correlated lower-dimensional product case obstructing uniform horocycle correlation bounds.
